cabinet install: SCOPE OF WORK — CABINET INSTALL  LABOR & MATERIALS (CABINETS & HARDWARE INCLUDED)  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The cabinet contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and materials required  to complete the cabinet scope outlined below. The contractor shall furnish all cabinets (kitchen,  bath vanities, laundry, mudroom, closets, and any other locations shown on plans), cabinet  hardware (knobs, pulls, hinges, drawer slides, soft-close mechanisms), fillers, toe kicks, crown  and light-rail molding, shims, fasteners, caulk, and all other installation materials. The contractor  is responsible for layout, field measurement, delivery, installation, and coordination with drywall,  plumbing, electrical, appliance, countertop, tile, and finish trades throughout the cabinet phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural drawings, cabinet shop drawings, and approved selection list prior  to fabrication and installation  •   Perform field measurements prior to cabinet fabrication to verify wall layout, ceiling  heights, plumbing/electrical rough-in locations, and appliance dimensions  •   Confirm cabinet layout, configuration, and hardware selections with owner/GC prior to  fabrication  •   Coordinate with plumbing contractor for sink base cutouts and fixture alignment  •   Coordinate with electrical contractor for under-cabinet lighting, outlet, and appliance  rough-in locations  •   Coordinate with appliance supplier for all appliance dimensions, clearances, and cutout  requirements  •   Coordinate with countertop fabricator for template timing, overhang, and support  requirements  •   Coordinate with tile contractor for backsplash timing and cabinet-to-tile transitions  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. CABINET FABRICATION & DELIVERY  •   Fabricate or procure cabinets per approved selection list, shop drawings, and field  measurements  •   Verify cabinet quality, f
concretedriveway: SCOPE OF WORK — CONCRETE DRIVEWAY  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The concrete contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, permitting, and  materials required to complete the concrete driveway scope outlined below. The contractor shall  furnish all form boards, stakes, fill / base material, reinforcement (wire mesh or rebar per plans),  vapor barrier (if required), concrete, curing compound, control joint materials, and all other  installation materials. The contractor shall pull the Right-of-Way (ROW) permit and all other  permits required for the driveway work. The contractor is responsible for layout, installation,  inspections, and coordination with site, utility, landscaping, and finish trades throughout the  driveway phase.  1. PERMITS & COORDINATION  •   Pull Right-of-Way (ROW) permit with jurisdiction having authority (city, county, or TxDOT  as applicable)  •   Pull any additional driveway or concrete permits required by jurisdiction  •   Submit driveway location, dimensions, culvert requirements, and apron details as  required for permit approval  •   Schedule and coordinate all required inspections (pre-pour / form & reinforcement  inspection, final inspection)  •   Review architectural, civil, and site drawings prior to installation  •   Verify driveway layout, width, thickness, and connection to street/curb per approved  plans and permit  •   Call for utility locates (811 / one-call) prior to excavation; do not dig until utilities are  marked  •   Coordinate with grading contractor to verify subgrade is established  •   Coordinate with plumbing, electrical, and low-voltage contractors for any sleeves or  crossings under driveway  •   Coordinate with landscaping and irrigation contractors for tie-ins and protection  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. SITE PREPARATION  •   Excavate and grade driveway area to required depth per plans and permit
countertop: SCOPE OF WORK — COUNTERTOPS  LABOR, FABRICATION & SLAB PROCUREMENT  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The countertop contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, templating,  fabrication, edge profiling, polishing, delivery, and installation required to complete the  countertop scope outlined below. The owner/GC shall select slab(s) from a stone yard /  supplier; the contractor shall procure the owner-selected slab(s), fabricate to approved shop  drawings, and install. The contractor shall furnish all fabrication materials, seam adhesive,  sealants, install hardware, and installation consumables. The contractor is responsible for  coordination with cabinet, plumbing, electrical, tile, and finish trades throughout the countertop  phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural drawings, cabinet shop drawings, and approved selection list prior  to template  •   Visit owner/GC-selected slab(s) at supplier yard; confirm slab reservation and verify  material matches approved selection  •   Procure owner-selected slab(s) from supplier; coordinate delivery to fabrication shop  •   Coordinate template timing after cabinets are fully installed and level  •   Coordinate with plumbing contractor for sink type, location, and fixture hole placement  •   Coordinate with electrical contractor for outlet locations, cooktop cutouts, and any  penetrations  •   Coordinate with cabinet contractor for support requirements at overhangs, islands, and  peninsulas  •   Confirm edge profile, seam locations, overhang dimensions, and backsplash height with  owner/GC prior to fabrication  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. COUNTERTOP LOCATIONS  •   Kitchen — all perimeter countertops, island, and peninsula per plans  •   Kitchen — full-height slab backsplash (same material as counter) at all kitchen wall  locations per plans  •   All bathroom vanities (master, secondary, and powder room) per pla
drywall: SCOPE OF WORK — DRYWALL  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The drywall contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and materials required  to complete the drywall scope outlined below. The contractor shall furnish all drywall, Hardie  backer board, joint tape, joint compound (mud), corner bead, fasteners, caulk / sealants, texture  material, and related accessories. The contractor is responsible for layout, installation, finishing,  and full coordination with framing, MEP, insulation, and paint trades throughout the drywall  phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural and structural drawings prior to drywall installation  •   Verify framing, insulation, and MEP rough-ins are complete and inspected  •   Verify all blocking and backing is in place for cabinets, handrails, fixtures, TVs, and other  finish items  •   Coordinate with MEP trades for cutouts, access panels, and finish-out requirements  •   Coordinate with paint contractor on finish quality expectations and lighting conditions  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  •   Schedule and coordinate any required inspections  2. HARDIE BACKER BOARD — BATHROOMS & WET AREAS  •   Furnish and install Hardie backer board at all bathroom tub/shower surrounds and wet  wall locations per plans  •   Install Hardie backer per manufacturer specifications including proper fastener type,  spacing, and edge clearances  •   Tape and seal all Hardie backer seams and corners with alkali-resistant mesh tape and  thin-set as required  •   Coordinate Hardie backer installation with tile contractor for substrate readiness  •   Maintain proper clearances from tub/shower pan flanges per manufacturer specifications  3. DRYWALL HANG  •   Furnish and install drywall on all walls and ceilings throughout the home per plans  •   Install moisture-resistant (green board) drywall at all bathrooms, laundry, and high-  humidity are
electrical: SCOPE OF WORK — ELECTRICAL  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The electrical contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and materials required  to complete the electrical scope outlined below. The contractor shall furnish all wire (including  the in-slab concrete-encased ground electrode / UFER ground wire), conduit, boxes, panels,  breakers, switches, receptacles, device plates, recessed can lights (LED), smoke/CO detectors,  exhaust fan rough-ins, disconnects, and all other rough and trim materials except as noted in  exclusions. The owner/general contractor shall supply only decorative sconces, decorative light  fixtures (pendants, chandeliers, vanity lights, etc.), and ceiling fans; all such items shall be  installed by the electrical contractor. The contractor is responsible for layout, installation, testing,  and coordination with foundation, framing, plumbing, HVAC, drywall, and finish trades  throughout the electrical phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural, electrical, and structural drawings prior to installation  •   Verify device locations, elevations, and rough-in dimensions per plans and owner walk-  through  •   Conduct pre-rough electrical walk-through with owner/GC to confirm switch, outlet, data,  and fixture locations  •   Coordinate with plumbing, HVAC, and low-voltage trades to avoid conflicts and maintain  required clearances  •   Coordinate with framing contractor for any required drilling, notching, and blocking  •   Coordinate with utility company for service connection, meter location, and temporary  power  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  •   Pull all required electrical permits and schedule all required inspections (rough, final, and  any specialty inspections)  2. IN-SLAB GROUND WIRE (UFER / CONCRETE-ENCASED ELECTRODE)  •   Furnish and install concrete-encased grounding electrode (UFER ground) wire in  foundati
environmental: SCOPE OF WORK — ENVIRONMENTAL / SITE PROTECTION  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The environmental/site protection contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination,  and materials required to complete the scope outlined below. The contractor shall furnish all silt  fence materials (fabric, posts, sock, plastic post caps), chain link tree-protection fencing (posts,  fabric, ties), mulch, and 5.5" bull rock for the construction entry. The contractor is also  responsible for tree removal and haul-off of felled trees as identified on plans. The contractor is  responsible for layout, installation, and coordination with the general contractor and other trades  throughout the project.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural, civil, and tree-survey/landscape plans prior to installation  •   Verify locations of silt fence, tree protection fencing, construction entry, and trees to be  removed vs. preserved per plans  •   Coordinate with general contractor on mobilization, staging, and access  •   Coordinate with surveyor and civil engineer as needed to confirm limits of disturbance  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  •   Schedule and coordinate any required municipal/SWPPP inspections  2. TREE REMOVAL  •   Fell all trees identified for removal on approved plans  •   Cut, limb, and section trees for removal as required  •   Haul off all felled trees, limbs, and associated debris from site  •   Protect adjacent trees, structures, utilities, and neighboring property during removal  operations  •   Verify no protected or preserved trees are removed; any questionable trees to be  confirmed with GC prior to cutting  •   Provide safe work practices, PPE, and comply with all applicable OSHA and arborist  safety standards  3. TREE PROTECTION FENCING (CHAIN LINK)  •   Furnish and install chain link fencing around all trees identified for protection on plans  •   Install fe
fencing: SCOPE OF WORK — WOOD FENCING  LABOR + CONCRETE (OWNER SUPPLIES ALL OTHER MATERIALS)  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The fencing contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and concrete / Post-  Crete required to set fence posts for the wood fencing scope outlined below. The owner/GC  shall supply all fence materials including posts, rails, pickets, gate frames, gate hardware  (hinges, latches, drop rods), fasteners, and any stain or sealer. The contractor is responsible for  layout, installation, and coordination with grading, landscaping, and irrigation contractors  throughout the fencing phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural and site drawings, including plot plan and fence layout, prior to  installation  •   Verify property lines, setbacks, and fence location with owner/GC prior to digging  •   Receive and inspect all owner/GC-supplied fence materials; notify GC of any shortages,  damage, or defects prior to installation  •   Call for utility locates (811 / one-call) prior to digging; do not dig until utilities are marked  •   Coordinate with grading contractor to verify grade is established prior to fence install  •   Coordinate with irrigation contractor to avoid damage to sprinkler lines  •   Coordinate gate locations, swing direction, and hardware locations with owner/GC  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. LAYOUT & POST SETTING  •   Lay out fence line per plans; string line and mark post locations at proper spacing  •   Dig post holes to required depth and diameter per code and wind-load requirements  •   Set posts plumb in concrete / Post-Crete (supplied by contractor); crown concrete at top  to shed water  •   Verify post height and alignment along fence line  •   Allow concrete to cure per manufacturer specifications prior to loading with rails/pickets  •   Backfill and tamp around post bases as needed  3. RAILS & PICKETS  •   Install top, midd
foundation: SCOPE OF WORK — FOUNDATION  LABOR & MATERIALS (PARTIAL)  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The foundation contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, and coordination required to  complete the foundation scope as outlined below. The foundation contractor shall furnish the  following materials: concrete, rebar, fill dirt, poly (vapor barrier) plastic, and form boards. All  other materials (including anchor bolts, hold-downs, embeds, plumbing/electrical stub-out  materials, and any specialty items) will be supplied by the owner/general contractor unless  otherwise noted. The foundation contractor is responsible for layout, installation, and full  coordination with other trades throughout the foundation phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review and interpret architectural, structural, and civil/site drawings prior to foundation  work  •   Establish and verify building layout, including corners, offsets, bearing points, and  elevations  •   Coordinate with surveyor for benchmark, setbacks, and building corners as required  •   Coordinate with MEP (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) trades for under-slab rough-ins,  sleeves, and penetration locations prior to pour  •   Coordinate with framing contractor for anchor bolt layout, hold-down locations, and plate  line elevations  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  •   Schedule and coordinate all required inspections (excavation, form/rebar, pre-pour, etc.)  2. SITE PREPARATION & EXCAVATION  •   Excavate footings, grade beams, and slab area to required depths per structural plans  •   Remove spoils from footing and grade beam excavation; stockpile or haul off per GC  direction  •   Provide, place, and compact fill dirt as required to bring pad to design elevation  •   Compact subgrade and fill in lifts per geotechnical/structural requirements  •   Fine-grade pad to receive vapor barrier and reinforcement  •   Protect excavation from water intrusion and
framing: SCOPE OF WORK — FRAMING  LABOR ONLY  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The framing contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, and coordination required to complete the  framing scope as outlined below. All materials will be supplied by the owner/general contractor. The  framing contractor is responsible for layout, installation, and full coordination with other trades  throughout the framing phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review and interpret architectural and structural drawings prior to framing  •   Establish and verify building layout, including plate lines, bearing points, and shear wall  locations  •   Coordinate with foundation contractor to verify slab/foundation is ready for framing  •   Coordinate with MEP (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) trades for blocking, framing openings,  and penetration locations  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. FLOOR FRAMING  •   Install floor plates, rim joists, and sill plates per plans  •   Frame floor systems including joists, engineered wood members (TJI/LVL), and blocking as  required  •   Install subfloor sheathing (materials provided) with proper fastening per code  •   Frame all openings for stairs, mechanical chases, and other penetrations  •   Install blocking and bridging as required by structural drawings  3. WALL FRAMING  •   Layout and frame all exterior and interior bearing and non-bearing walls per plans  •   Install bottom plates, top plates, and double top plates  •   Frame all window and door openings with proper headers, king studs, jack studs, and cripples  •   Install shear wall framing and blocking per structural drawings  •   Frame all interior partition walls, closets, and chases  •   Install backing and blocking for cabinets, fixtures, handrails, and other finish items as indicated  •   Install wall sheathing with proper nailing patterns per shear wall schedule (materials provided)  4. ROOF FRAMING  •   Frame roof sys
hvac: SCOPE OF WORK — HVAC  LABOR & MATERIALS (FULL SYSTEM)  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The HVAC contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and materials required to  complete the HVAC scope outlined below. The contractor shall furnish all equipment  (condensers, air handlers, furnaces, coils, line sets, thermostats), ductwork (supply, return, and  trunk lines), registers, grilles, diffusers, bath exhaust fans, dryer vents, fresh air intakes,  refrigerant, condensate drainage, and all other rough and trim materials. The contractor is  responsible for design, load calculations (Manual J/S/D as required), layout, installation, startup,  testing, balancing, and coordination with framing, plumbing, electrical, insulation, drywall, and  finish trades throughout the HVAC phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural and mechanical drawings prior to installation  •   Perform Manual J load calculations, Manual S equipment selection, and Manual D duct  design as required by code and energy compliance  •   Verify equipment locations, duct routing, and register/grille locations per plans and load  calculations  •   Coordinate with framing contractor for mechanical chases, soffits, drops, platforms, and  required structural openings  •   Coordinate with plumbing and electrical trades to avoid conflicts and maintain required  clearances  •   Coordinate with electrical contractor for all required HVAC power, disconnects, and  thermostat wiring  •   Coordinate with roofer for all roof penetrations (vents, flues, exhaust) and flashing  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  •   Pull all required mechanical permits and schedule all required inspections (rough, final,  and any specialty inspections)  2. EQUIPMENT  •   Furnish and install all condensers / outdoor units on properly sized and leveled pads  •   Furnish and install all air handlers / furnaces at designated locations (attic,
insulation batt: SCOPE OF WORK — INSULATION (BATT)  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The insulation contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and materials  required to complete the batt insulation scope outlined below. The contractor shall furnish all  batt insulation, vapor retarders, fasteners, and accessories. The contractor is responsible for  layout, installation, and coordination with framing, MEP, and drywall trades throughout the  insulation phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural, structural, and energy compliance documents prior to installation  •   Verify framing is complete, inspected, and ready for insulation  •   Verify all MEP rough-ins (electrical, plumbing, HVAC, low-voltage) are complete and  inspected prior to insulation  •   Coordinate with GC on access, scheduling, and drywall readiness  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  •   Schedule and coordinate required insulation inspections  2. EXTERIOR WALL INSULATION — R-15 BATTS  •   Furnish and install R-15 batt insulation in all exterior wall cavities  •   Cut batts to fit cavity; avoid compression and ensure full depth of cavity is filled  •   Cut and fit around electrical boxes, plumbing, wiring, and other penetrations; do not  compress or stuff  •   Split batts around wiring and plumbing where required to maintain full R-value  •   Install insulation behind electrical boxes, headers, corners, and T-walls before drywall  backing is installed  •   Ensure batts are in continuous contact with air barrier on exterior side  3. CEILING / ATTIC INSULATION — R-38 BATTS  •   Furnish and install R-38 batt insulation at all ceiling areas below vented attic space  •   Install baffles at eave vents to maintain clear airflow from soffit vents  •   Install insulation dams at attic access hatches, mechanical platforms, and recessed  fixtures as required  •   Ensure full coverage with no gaps, voids, or
insulation sprayfoam: SCOPE OF WORK — INSULATION (SPRAY FOAM)  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The insulation contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and materials  required to complete the spray foam insulation scope outlined below. The contractor shall  furnish all spray foam material, primers, fasteners, and accessories. Foam type (open-cell,  closed-cell, or hybrid) and thicknesses shall be installed per approved plans and specifications.  The contractor is responsible for layout, installation, and coordination with framing, MEP, and  drywall trades throughout the insulation phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural, structural, and energy compliance documents to determine foam  type, locations, and required thicknesses per plans  •   Verify framing is complete, inspected, and ready for insulation  •   Verify all MEP rough-ins (electrical, plumbing, HVAC, low-voltage) are complete and  inspected prior to foam application  •   Coordinate with GC on access, scheduling, ventilation during spray, and drywall  readiness  •   Confirm occupant/trade re-entry times per manufacturer specifications  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  •   Schedule and coordinate required insulation inspections  2. SITE PREPARATION  •   Mask, cover, and protect all windows, doors, finished surfaces, mechanical equipment,  and adjacent work from overspray  •   Protect all HVAC equipment, registers, and ductwork from overspray and foam intrusion  •   Verify ambient and substrate temperature and moisture conditions are within  manufacturer-specified range prior to application  •   Provide adequate ventilation during and after spray application per manufacturer and  OSHA requirements  •   Post appropriate signage restricting site access during and after spray application per re-  entry requirements  3. SPRAY FOAM APPLICATION  •   Foam type, thickness, and locations shall be installed per
landscape: SCOPE OF WORK — LANDSCAPE & IRRIGATION  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The landscape contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and materials  required to complete the landscape and irrigation scope outlined below. The contractor shall  furnish all irrigation components (controller, valves, pipe, fittings, heads, drip lines, wire,  backflow preventer, sensors), plants (trees, shrubs, perennials, groundcover, sod) per approved  selection list, topsoil and soil amendments, landscape fabric, edging, mulch, gravel per  approved selection, and all other installation materials. Owner/GC will select plant types and  gravel type from contractor's proposal. The contractor is responsible for design coordination,  installation, startup, testing, plant establishment, warranty replacement, and coordination with  grading, irrigation meter, driveway, fencing, and finish trades throughout the landscape phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural, civil, grading, and landscape drawings prior to installation  •   Provide plant selection proposal and gravel options for owner/GC approval prior to  procurement  •   Confirm final plant list, sizes, quantities, and gravel selection with owner/GC in writing  prior to order  •   Call for utility locates (811 / one-call) prior to trenching or digging; do not dig until utilities  are marked  •   Coordinate with site work / grading contractor for final grade and drainage  •   Coordinate with concrete / driveway contractor for any sleeves or crossings under  hardscape  •   Coordinate with plumbing / utility contractors for irrigation water service connection and  backflow preventer location  •   Coordinate with electrical contractor for irrigation controller power and any landscape  lighting power (if applicable)  •   Coordinate with fencing contractor to avoid damage to posts and gates  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor
outdoorkitchen: SCOPE OF WORK — OUTDOOR KITCHEN  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The outdoor kitchen contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and materials  required to complete the outdoor kitchen structure scope outlined below. The contractor shall  furnish all block, mortar, rebar, concrete, substrate / backer board, stucco and/or stone veneer  materials per plans, fasteners, sealants, and all other structural and finish materials. The  contractor is responsible for layout, structural construction, exterior finish, appliance cutouts,  utility tie-ins from stub locations, and coordination with plumbing, gas, electrical, countertop, and  appliance trades throughout the outdoor kitchen phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural, structural, and site drawings prior to installation  •   Verify outdoor kitchen location, dimensions, layout, and design per approved plans  •   Receive owner/GC-supplied appliance specifications and cut sheets prior to construction  •   Confirm appliance cutout dimensions, clearances, and ventilation requirements per  manufacturer specifications  •   Coordinate with plumbing contractor for water and drain stub locations  •   Coordinate with gas contractor for gas stub location and capacity  •   Coordinate with electrical contractor for power stub locations and outlet placements  •   Coordinate with countertop fabricator for template timing and support requirements  •   Coordinate with landscape, hardscape, and pool contractors on adjacent work  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  •   Pull any required permits and schedule inspections as required by jurisdiction  2. FOUNDATION / BASE  •   Verify existing slab or base is adequate for outdoor kitchen structure, or install  footing/slab per plans and engineering  •   Pour concrete footing/pad as required by plans and soil conditions  •   Install rebar reinforcement per plans  •   Verif
paint: SCOPE OF WORK — PAINT & STAIN  LABOR & MATERIALS (INTERIOR & EXTERIOR)  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The paint contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and materials required to  complete the interior and exterior paint and stain scope outlined below. The contractor shall  furnish all paint, primer, stain, sealer, topcoat, caulk, spackle, putty, wood filler, sandpaper,  masking materials, drop cloths, and all other application supplies. Paint products, colors, and  sheens shall be per approved plans, specifications, and owner/GC selection list. The contractor  is responsible for surface preparation, application, coordination with drywall, trim, cabinet, and  finish trades, and final punch-list throughout the paint phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural drawings, finish schedule, and approved color/sheen selection list  prior to work  •   Confirm paint product (brand, line, color, sheen) for each location with owner/GC prior to  order and application  •   Coordinate with drywall contractor to verify texture and substrate are ready for  primer/paint  •   Coordinate with trim contractor to verify trim is installed and ready for caulk/fill/finish  •   Coordinate with cabinet contractor for stain/finish timing (if cabinets are site-finished per  plans)  •   Coordinate with flooring contractor for floor protection and sequence  •   Coordinate with siding, roofing, and exterior trim contractors for exterior paint readiness  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. INTERIOR SURFACE PREPARATION  •   Mask, cover, and protect all floors, cabinets, countertops, windows, fixtures, and  adjacent surfaces prior to paint  •   Caulk all trim joints, gaps between trim and wall, base-to-wall, casing-to-wall, and all  other transitions as required for a paint-grade finish  •   Fill all nail holes in trim, doors, and casing with paintable spackle/wood filler; sand  smooth  •
plumbing: SCOPE OF WORK — PLUMBING  LABOR & MATERIALS (INCLUDING FIXTURES)  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The plumbing contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and materials  required to complete the plumbing scope outlined below. The contractor shall furnish all rough-  in materials (pipe, fittings, valves, hangers, primers, glues, sleeves, water heater, and related  items), all top-out materials, and all fixtures per approved selection list. The contractor is  responsible for layout, installation, testing, and coordination with foundation, framing, HVAC,  electrical, drywall, tile, and finish trades throughout the plumbing phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural, structural, civil, and plumbing drawings prior to installation  •   Verify fixture locations, elevations, and rough-in dimensions per plans and fixture cut  sheets  •   Coordinate with foundation contractor for under-slab rough-in timing prior to vapor  barrier and pour  •   Coordinate with framing contractor for penetrations, wall depths, blocking, and chase  locations  •   Coordinate with HVAC and electrical trades to avoid conflicts and maintain required  clearances  •   Coordinate with tile and cabinet contractors for valve, drain, and stub-out locations  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  •   Pull all required plumbing permits and schedule all required inspections (under-slab,  rough, top-out, final)  2. FIXTURE SELECTIONS  •   All fixtures shall be supplied and installed per the approved fixture selection list signed  off by owner/GC  •   Contractor shall confirm fixture availability, lead times, and rough-in requirements at time  of selection approval  •   Any fixture changes or substitutions after approved selection list require written change  order  •   Contractor to receive, inspect, store, and protect all fixtures through installation  3. UNDER-SLAB ROUGH-IN  •   Install all under-slab drain
pool: SCOPE OF WORK — POOL  GUNITE / SHOTCRETE WITH PLASTER FINISH  POOL + DECK + COPING | LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The pool contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, permitting, and materials  required to complete the pool scope outlined below. The scope includes pool shell construction  (gunite/shotcrete with plaster finish), pool plumbing and equipment, pool electrical within the  pool scope, tile and coping, and pool deck. The contractor shall furnish all excavation services,  steel/rebar, gunite/shotcrete, plumbing, pool electrical, tile, coping, deck material, plaster, pool  equipment (pump, filter, heater if specified, sanitizer, controls), chemicals for startup, and all  other installation materials. The contractor is responsible for layout, installation, startup, testing,  and coordination with general contractor, foundation, landscape, fencing, and utility trades  throughout the pool phase.  1. PERMITS & COORDINATION  •   Pull all required pool permits (pool construction, electrical, plumbing) with jurisdiction  having authority  •   Review architectural, civil, and pool engineering drawings prior to installation  •   Verify pool location, dimensions, depth, and features per approved plans  •   Call for utility locates (811 / one-call) prior to excavation; do not dig until utilities are  marked  •   Coordinate with GC on site access, crane/truck access, and construction entry  •   Coordinate with landscape contractor on bed locations, plantings near pool, and  irrigation sleeves  •   Coordinate with fencing contractor on pool safety barrier per code  •   Coordinate with plumbing contractor for water fill line tie-in and any required sanitary  connections  •   Coordinate with electrical contractor for sub-panel power to pool equipment, bonding,  and any shared circuits  •   Coordinate with gas contractor if pool heater is gas-fired  •   Attend site meetings as required by
roofing: SCOPE OF WORK — ROOFING  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The roofing contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, and materials required  to complete the roofing scope outlined below. The contractor shall furnish all dry-in materials  (synthetic underlayment, ice & water shield, felt as applicable), drip edge, starter strip, shingles,  ridge cap, valley metal, pipe boots/flashings, nails/fasteners, and all other roofing materials. The  contractor is responsible for layout, installation, proper flashing integration, and coordination  with framing, HVAC, plumbing, and siding trades throughout the roofing phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural and structural drawings prior to installation  •   Verify roof decking, sheathing, and framing are complete, fastened, and ready to receive  roofing  •   Coordinate with HVAC contractor for all roof penetrations (vents, flues, exhaust  terminations)  •   Coordinate with plumbing contractor for all plumbing vent penetrations  •   Coordinate with electrical contractor for service mast, solar conduit, or other penetrations  (if applicable)  •   Coordinate with siding and exterior trim contractors for proper flashing integration at wall  intersections  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  •   Schedule and coordinate any required roofing inspections  2. DRY-IN / UNDERLAYMENT  •   Furnish and install synthetic underlayment (or felt per plans) across entire roof deck  •   Furnish and install ice & water shield at all eaves, valleys, rakes, penetrations, and other  vulnerable areas per code and manufacturer specifications  •   Lap and fasten underlayment per manufacturer specifications to achieve proper weather  protection  •   Seal and flash all penetrations (plumbing vents, HVAC vents, exhaust ducts, electrical  mast) during dry-in  •   Install drip edge at all eaves and rakes per code; proper lap with underlaym
sitework: SCOPE OF WORK — SITE WORK  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The site work contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, coordination, equipment, and  materials required to complete the site work scope outlined below. The contractor shall furnish  all fill dirt, base material (if required), and any other materials needed to bring the site to rough-  graded condition ready for foundation. The contractor is responsible for tree removal (fell, haul  off, and stump grinding), clearing, grubbing, excavation, fill placement, rough grading, and  coordination with surveyor, environmental/site protection, foundation, and utility contractors  throughout the site work phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural, civil, grading, and site drawings prior to work  •   Verify locations of trees to be removed vs. preserved per approved plans  •   Verify building footprint, driveway location, and finish floor elevation with surveyor and  GC  •   Call for utility locates (811 / one-call) prior to excavation; do not dig until utilities are  marked  •   Coordinate with environmental/site protection contractor so tree protection, silt fence,  and construction entry are in place prior to clearing  •   Coordinate with foundation contractor on pad elevation, compaction, and readiness  requirements  •   Coordinate with utility contractors for any required service trenching or tie-in work  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  •   Schedule and coordinate any required jurisdiction inspections (clearing, grading,  SWPPP)  2. TREE REMOVAL  •   Fell all trees identified for removal on approved plans  •   Cut, limb, and section trees for removal as required  •   Haul off all felled trees, limbs, and associated debris from site  •   Grind all stumps below finish grade (minimum 6 inches below grade or per plans)  •   Remove or dispose of stump grindings per GC direction  •   Protect adjacent trees
survey initial: SCOPE OF WORK — SURVEY (INITIAL)  LOT CORNERS + FOUNDATION CORNERS + ELEVATIONS  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The surveyor shall provide all labor, supervision, equipment, instruments, stakes, ribbon, and  materials required to complete the initial survey scope outlined below. This scope includes  staking the four (4) corners of the lot, staking the four (4) corners of the foundation/slab, and  establishing elevations for the foundation and yard. The surveyor shall be a Registered  Professional Land Surveyor (RPLS) licensed in the State of Texas. The surveyor is responsible  for coordination with the general contractor, site work contractor, and foundation contractor  throughout the survey phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review recorded plat, legal description, architectural drawings, and site/grading plans  prior to field work  •   Research existing monuments, benchmarks, and any prior surveys of the property  •   Coordinate with GC and foundation contractor on timing for each survey phase  •   Provide signed and sealed survey drawings and reports per RPLS licensing  requirements  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. LOT CORNER SURVEY (PROPERTY CORNERS)  •   Locate and verify all four (4) corners of the property per recorded plat and legal  description  •   Set or verify existing property corner monuments (iron rods, capped pins, or approved  markers)  •   Stake and flag all four corners with visible markers for GC and site contractors  •   Verify property lines, setbacks, and easements per plat  •   Provide signed and sealed sketch or plat indicating property corners and setbacks  3. FOUNDATION CORNER SURVEY (SLAB CORNERS)  •   Stake all four (4) corners of the foundation/slab per architectural drawings and approved  site plan  •   Verify building placement complies with setbacks, easements, and approved site plan  •   Mark offsets as needed for foundation contracto
survey perviouscoverage: SCOPE OF WORK — SURVEY (PERVIOUS COVERAGE)  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The surveyor shall provide all labor, supervision, equipment, instruments, and materials  required to complete the pervious coverage survey scope outlined below. This survey shall be  performed at the end of the project after all impervious surfaces (foundation, driveway,  sidewalks, patios, and any other hardscape) have been installed. The surveyor shall be a  Registered Professional Land Surveyor (RPLS) licensed in the State of Texas. The surveyor is  responsible for coordination with the general contractor and jurisdiction requirements throughout  the survey.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review recorded plat, legal description, approved site plan, and jurisdiction pervious  coverage requirements prior to field work  •   Review as-built conditions of all impervious surfaces (home foundation, driveway,  sidewalks, patios, and other hardscape)  •   Coordinate with GC on timing after all exterior construction is complete  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. PERVIOUS COVERAGE SURVEY  •   Measure and document the total area of all impervious surfaces on the lot, including but  not limited to: home foundation footprint, driveway, sidewalks, patios, pool decks (if any),  hardscape, and any other impervious improvements  •   Calculate impervious coverage as a percentage of total lot area  •   Calculate pervious coverage as a percentage of total lot area  •   Verify pervious coverage percentage meets jurisdiction requirements and approved site  plan  •   Prepare pervious coverage survey drawing showing all impervious areas clearly  delineated and dimensioned  •   Provide signed and sealed pervious coverage survey / report suitable for submittal to  jurisdiction  3. GENERAL REQUIREMENTS  •   All survey work shall comply with Texas Board of Professional Land Surveying (TBPLS)  standards and RPLS licensing re
tile: SCOPE OF WORK — TILE  LABOR & PARTIAL MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The tile contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, and coordination required to complete the  tile scope outlined below. The contractor shall furnish thinset mortar and all installation  consumables (spacers, trowels, mixing tools, cleaning supplies, etc.). The owner/general  contractor shall supply all tile, grout, and Schluter waterproofing/trim products. The tile  contractor is responsible for layout, installation, waterproofing, grouting, sealing, and  coordination with plumbing, drywall, and finish trades throughout the tile phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural drawings, finish schedules, and tile specifications prior to  installation  •   Verify substrate readiness: drywall, Hardie backer, and subfloor are complete, flat,  plumb, and ready for tile  •   Verify all plumbing rough-ins, valves, drains, and niches are properly located and ready  •   Confirm tile layout, pattern, and starting points with owner/GC prior to installation  •   Receive, inspect, and inventory tile, grout, and Schluter materials supplied by owner/GC;  notify GC of any shortages, damage, or defects prior to installation  •   Coordinate with plumbing contractor on fixture trim-out timing  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. TILE LOCATIONS  •   All bathrooms (master, secondary, and Jack-and-Jill as applicable)  •   Powder room  •   Laundry room  •   Any additional locations where tile is called for on approved plans and finish schedule  •   Includes floors, wall tile, shower surrounds, tub surrounds, niches, curbs, thresholds,  and accent areas as shown on plans  3. SURFACE PREPARATION & WATERPROOFING  •   Verify substrate is clean, flat, plumb, and within tolerances required for tile installation  •   Install Schluter Kerdi (or equivalent) waterproofing membrane at all shower and tub  surrounds, including wall
trashhauloff: SCOPE OF WORK — TRASH HAUL-OFF  LABOR & MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The trash haul-off contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, equipment (trucks, dumpsters,  bins, loaders as needed), and disposal services required to pick up and haul off construction  trash and debris from the project site. This scope covers both on-demand (per-visit) service and  an optional recurring service on a weekly, biweekly, or monthly schedule selected by owner/GC.  The contractor is responsible for proper disposal at approved facilities and coordination with the  general contractor and active trades throughout the project.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Coordinate with GC on site access, pickup locations, and preferred staging areas for  debris  •   Coordinate with GC on scheduling of each haul-off visit (on-demand or recurring)  •   Comply with any site-specific rules regarding access hours, noise restrictions, and  neighborhood considerations  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. SCOPE OF HAUL-OFF  •   Pick up all construction trash and debris on site, including but not limited to: lumber  scraps, drywall scraps, cardboard, packaging, insulation scraps, roofing material scraps,  flooring scraps, tile/stone scraps, empty paint/caulk containers (dry), and general  construction debris  •   Pick up trash from designated staging areas, dumpsters, or piles  •   Load all debris into trucks or containers for haul-off  •   Sweep and clean up pickup areas after haul-off to leave site reasonably clean  •   Haul all debris to approved disposal, recycling, or transfer facilities  •   Dispose of all materials per local regulations and environmental requirements  •   Provide disposal documentation / tickets to GC upon request  3. GENERAL REQUIREMENTS  •   All work shall comply with applicable local codes, environmental regulations, and  jurisdiction disposal requirements  •   Contractor shall carry proper i
trim doors: SCOPE OF WORK — TRIM & INTERIOR DOORS  LABOR ONLY  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The trim contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, and coordination required to complete  the interior door hanging and trim scope outlined below. All materials (interior doors, jambs,  casing, base, door hardware, fasteners, caulk, and shims) will be supplied by the owner/general  contractor. The trim contractor is responsible for layout, installation, and coordination with  drywall, paint, flooring, and finish trades throughout the trim phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural drawings, door schedule, and finish schedule prior to installation  •   Receive and inspect all owner/GC-supplied doors, jambs, casing, base, and hardware;  notify GC of any shortages, damage, or defects prior to installation  •   Verify rough openings are plumb, square, and sized correctly for doors supplied  •   Verify drywall and flooring are complete (or coordinate install sequence per GC  direction)  •   Coordinate with paint contractor on finish timing and sequence  •   Coordinate with flooring contractor on base installation timing (before or after flooring per  GC)  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. INTERIOR DOOR HANGING  •   Hang all interior doors throughout the home per door schedule and plans (pre-hung and  slab doors as supplied)  •   Install jambs plumb, level, and square; shim as needed  •   Secure jambs to framing with proper fasteners  •   Verify proper reveal (gap) around door slab on all three sides  •   Adjust and align doors so they swing freely, latch properly, and remain in position when  left open  •   Install door hardware (hinges, knobs/levers, latches, locksets, privacy locks, door stops)  supplied by owner/GC  •   Install pocket door and barn door hardware and track systems where specified on plans  (hardware supplied by GC)  •   Install bi-fold, bypass, and closet doors per plans  3.
woodfloor: SCOPE OF WORK — WOOD FLOORING  LABOR & PARTIAL MATERIALS  New Construction | Single-Family Home | Mix of Materials  GENERAL CONDITIONS  The wood flooring contractor shall provide all labor, supervision, and coordination required to  complete the wood flooring scope outlined below. The contractor shall furnish all adhesive  (glue), installation consumables (trowels, spacers, moisture meters, etc.), and floor protection  product to cover the finished floor. The owner/general contractor shall supply all wood flooring  material. The contractor is responsible for layout, installation, and coordination with other trades  throughout the flooring phase.  1. LAYOUT & COORDINATION  •   Review architectural drawings, finish schedules, and flooring specifications prior to  installation  •   Verify substrate (concrete slab) is clean, flat, dry, and within manufacturer-specified  tolerances for flatness and moisture prior to install  •   Perform moisture and pH testing on slab per adhesive manufacturer specifications and  document results  •   Confirm flooring layout, starting points, direction of run, and transitions with owner/GC  prior to installation  •   Receive, inspect, and inventory wood flooring supplied by owner/GC; notify GC of any  shortages, damage, or defects prior to installation  •   Acclimate wood flooring on site per manufacturer specifications prior to install  •   Coordinate with GC on HVAC operation, climate conditions, and site readiness prior to  and during install  •   Attend site meetings as required by general contractor  2. WOOD FLOORING LOCATIONS  •   Install wood flooring throughout the home in all locations where wood is called for on  approved plans and finish schedule  •   Includes all associated transitions, thresholds, and edge details as shown on plans  3. SUBSTRATE PREPARATION  •   Inspect slab for flatness; flag any areas outside adhesive manufacturer tolerance to GC  prior to install  •   Grind high spots and fill low spots as required with a
